mainText:
Good morning. Here's your Breeze communications briefing for Tuesday, April 7th.

In Breeze news this morning: Breeze Airways officially launched nonstop service from Lincoln, Nebraska to Las Vegas yesterday, with Lincoln's local news covering the airline's arrival at the airport ahead of the first revenue flights, which are scheduled to begin this Wednesday, April 9th. Service will operate three times weekly on an Airbus A220, and Breeze is also offering its BreezeThru routing that connects Lincoln passengers onward to Orange County without a plane change, opening single-ticket access to both Las Vegas and Southern California from central Nebraska for the first time. Lincoln is the latest in a string of 2026 market activations for Breeze, adding another mid-size regional city to a network that has been growing steadily across underserved routes that legacy carriers have overlooked. No adverse coverage of the Lincoln launch is visible at this time, and the early press appears positive.

Now for industry and competitor news.

Traveltourister reported that Easter Monday recorded 5,029 total U.S. flight disruptions — 307 cancellations and 4,722 delays — making it the worst single day of the holiday travel weekend, with Delta Air Lines leading all carriers in cancellations, Atlanta's Hartsfield-Jackson ranking as the most disrupted hub in the nation, and Miami still absorbing nearly 200 delays this Tuesday morning as airlines work to reposition aircraft across the network. Breeze's secondary-airport model largely insulated its passengers from this hub cascade, and the communications and social teams may want to make that reliability contrast visible while Easter hub chaos is still dominating travel headlines.

The American Customer Satisfaction Index released an update to its 2025 airline rankings on Monday, covering major U.S. carriers across 21 performance benchmarks including ease of check-in, cabin cleanliness, loyalty program quality, and on-time arrivals, with Southwest, JetBlue, Alaska, Frontier, American, Delta, and United all posting improved scores in what the A.C.S.I. described as broadly positive industry momentum. Breeze does not appear in the A.C.S.I. rankings, which may warrant a conversation about commissioning independent third-party customer experience measurement — particularly if a public market or I.P.O. narrative is being built.

Time magazine reported Monday that T.S.A. officer call-out rates fell 43 percent after President Trump ordered agency employees paid during the ongoing D.H.S. shutdown, with security wait times at major hubs dropping from multi-hour peaks to under ten minutes at most airports. The Senate passed a stop-gap D.H.S. funding bill on April 5th that would fully restore T.S.A. operations, but the measure still awaits House action — leadership should assess whether any Breeze markets experienced disproportionate staffing gaps during the shutdown that would now benefit from proactive passenger outreach.

Traveltourister reported this week that airports in northern Italy — including Milan Linate, Venice Marco Polo, Bologna, and Treviso — began emergency jet fuel rationing, capping individual aircraft uplifts at just 2,000 liters per fill-up through at least April 9th, the latest acute symptom of the global supply crisis triggered by the Iran war's closure of the Strait of Hormuz, which has pushed U.S. jet fuel prices up roughly 95 percent since late February. Breeze's Airbus A220 fleet is among the most fuel-efficient narrowbody aircraft in operation today, and that structural cost advantage deserves explicit mention in any upcoming investor, media, or revenue management conversations as the broader industry absorbs a cost shock that shows no immediate sign of easing.

That's your briefing. Roger is standing by when you're ready to dive deeper or take action on any of these stories.
